macro_segments.title: The Statutory Fine of Fifty Shekel and the Inference of Additional Payments for Embarrassment and Devaluation

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Mishnah's Enumeration of Rape Payments and the Gemara's Opening Question
Abaye's Logical Challenge: Why Should the Fifty Shekel Be Identical Regardless of the Victim's Status?
Rabbi Zeira's Response: The Physical State Distinction Between a Raped and Unviolated Woman
Abaye's Counter-Analogy: The Slave Gored by an Ox Shows Fixed Amounts Regardless of Condition
Abaye's Scriptural Proof: The Fifty Shekel Compensates Only the Act of Intercourse, Not Associated Injuries
Rava's Alternative Scriptural Derivation Using the Pleasures of Cohabitation

macro_segments.title: The Beneficiary of the Monetary Payments: Establishing That Boshet and Pegam Go to the Father, Not the Girl

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: To Whom Do Boshet and Pegam Payments Go?
The Logical Objection: In Seduction Cases, Why Would the Girl Receive Payment If She Consented?
The Principle of Shevach Neurim: All Benefits from Youth Accrue to the Father
Rav Huna in the Name of Rav: The Daughter's Earnings Belong to the Father via Comparison to a Slave
The Gemara's Objection: The Verse on Vows Does Not Establish a General Principle of Paternal Control
The Resolution: Monetary Rights Cannot Be Derived from Ritual Obligations; Each Source Teaches a Specific Domain
The Fines Principle: Even the Fifty Shekel Fine Does Not Establish Paternal Rights to Injury Compensation
The Father's Control Rationale: The Father Controls Her Marriage, Virginity, and Shame, Thus Receives Associated Payments

macro_segments.title: The Valuation of Pegam (Devaluation): Using the Slave Market as the Standard for Assessing Monetary Damages

micro_segments.title: How Pegam Is Calculated: Comparing the Price of a Virgin Maidservant to a Non-Virgin
The Gemara's Clarification: The Valuation Assumes the Slave Is to Be Married to Another Slave
Tosafot's Difficulty: The Slave Market Price Does Not Reflect the Woman's Actual Loss in Social Standing
Tosafot's Proposed Solution: The Valuation Should Account for What a High-Status Woman Would Be Worth in the Slave Market

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Meir and the Sages: Sale and Fine as Mutually Exclusive Categories

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's Position: Where There Is Sale, There Is No Fine; Where There Is Fine, There Is No Sale
The Mishnaic Enumeration: A Minor Can Be Sold But Has No Fine; A Na'arah Has a Fine But Cannot Be Sold
The Sages' Disagreement: The Fine Applies Even to a Minor from Three Years and One Day Onward
Rabbi Meir's Scriptural Proof: The Verb 'She Shall Be' Indicates an Adult Woman Who Can Marry Herself
The Sages' Scriptural Proof: The Missing Letter Hei in Na'arah Indicates It Applies Even to a Minor
The Distinction Clarified: The Missing Hei Shows the Fine Applies to Minors, While the Full Spelling in Motzi Shem Ra Indicates Only Adults

macro_segments.title: Fines Versus Monetary Payments: The Legal Distinction and Its Consequences for Self-Admission

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Principle: One Who Admits to Seduction Pays Boshet and Pegam but Not the Fine
The Halakhic Rule: Fines Can Only Be Imposed by Court; Self-Admission Does Not Obligate Payment of a Fine
Examples from Other Domains: Theft, Goring, and Murder Show the Distinction Between Fines and Compensation
The Criterion for Distinguishing Fine from Compensation: If Payment Exceeds Actual Damages, It Is a Fine

macro_segments.title: The Case of Seduction Admission: Whether the Court Should Accept a Confession That Spreads Ill Repute

micro_segments.title: Rava's Distinction: Rape Admission Does Not Spread Ill Repute; Seduction Admission Does
Rabbi Shimon ben Yehuda's Position: The Court Should Not Accept Even Boshet and Pegam Admissions in Seduction Cases
The Gemara's Escalating Concerns: The Court Protects Against Family Shame Even If the Immediate Parties Consent
Tosafot's Distinction: The Restriction Applies Only When the Perpetrator Initiates; If the Victim Sues, He Must Pay

macro_segments.title: The Debate Over Half Damages for a Non-Habitual Goring Ox: Whether It Constitutes a Fine or Compensation

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's Position: Half Damages Is Monetary Compensation, Not a Fine
Rav Huna's Position: Half Damages Is a Fine Imposed to Encourage Care of Animals
The Textual Problem: The Mishnah's Language About Sharing the Loss Seems to Indicate Compensation, Not a Fine
The Resolution: The Mishnah Teaches Only About Depreciation of the Carcass, Not All Differences Between Tam and Muad
Additional Evidence: The Mishnah's List of Tam-Muad Differences Omits the Fine/Payment Distinction, Suggesting Both Are Payments
